title: The Reading List for the Next Layer of Money Work
subtitle: Books and resources for going deeper once the foundational practices are in place
excerpt: A curated reading list for people who have started money mindset work and want to go further. Organised by what each resource does well, with a note on what layer of the work each one addresses.

content.text: A short reading list for people who want to go deeper. These are not all money books in the traditional sense. They're books that address the identity, belief, and inner work layer that sits beneath financial behaviour. Listed by what each one does well, not ranked.
For the earning and receiving layer

Rich As F*ck by Amanda Frances. The clearest book on aligning your identity to a higher income level. Written for women, particularly self-employed women. Direct and practical. Good for the ceiling work.

We Should All Be Millionaires by Rachel Rodgers. Addresses the structural and identity barriers specific to women of colour, but the identity framework applies broadly. Strong on the link between self-worth and pricing.
For the belief and nervous system layer

The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. Not a money book. A thorough explanation of how the body stores emotional patterns and why talk-based approaches have limits. Useful context for understanding why somatic practices matter in money work.

You Are a Badass at Making Money by Jen Sincero. Accessible, practical, good on the 'I don't deserve' layer and the identity gap between where you are and where you want to be. Some readers find the tone irritating; if that's you, start elsewhere.
For the inheritance and family layer

It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn. About inherited family trauma broadly, not money specifically, but the framework for identifying and updating patterns that predate you is directly applicable. The most useful book for the ancestry and inheritance layer of money work.

Get Good with Money by Tiffany Aliche. Covers the practical financial layer: budgeting, debt, savings, and investing. The most accessible and non-shaming treatment available. Not an inner work book, but the practical foundation is necessary. Good companion to the inner work in this library rather than a replacement for it.
